text: AI-Advised Microkernel OS Simulator for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles
Autonomous underwater vehicles cannot depend on manual recovery once a mission is underway.
Navigation, sonar, depth control, and hull checks must complete inside tight timing windows.
Every scheduling decision affects battery draw, mission duration, and thermal load.
A logging or communication fault should not cascade into core vehicle control.
At depth, the OS must recover, degrade gracefully, or surface safely without operator access.
End-eval focus: prove the simulator handles timing, faults, resources, and evaluation metrics together.
Design and evaluate a simulated OS layer that protects mission-critical AUV processes.
Use task and system-state signals to estimate fault probability before damage spreads.
Let AI advise the scheduler, while deterministic kernel logic remains in control.
Include memory pressure, battery state, depth pressure, I/O waits, and survival mode.
Compare Hybrid, EDF, Static Priority, and Round Robin on the same seeded workload.
Final scope is a Python OS simulator, not a certified RTOS or hardware deployment.
The mid deck proposed modules; the final implementation connects them into a measurable system.
AI advisory, scheduling module, controlled fault injection, metrics, and simulation-based evaluation.
Microkernel-style simulator with task lifecycle, schedulers, resources, environment, BMS, CLI, and AI advisor.
Fresh benchmark run, JSON outputs, chart-ready KPIs, and deterministic tests across key modules.

text: The simulator separates mission dynamics, kernel control, resource management, AI advice, and monitoring.
SystemSimulator drives ticks, events, task lifecycle, environment, and survival phases.
Kernel admits tasks, dispatches scheduler decisions, requeues states, and records context switches.
ResourceManager controls memory, BMS energy drain, admission control, and pressure states.
AIAdvisor estimates risk and returns a priority boost without taking over kernel authority.
Metrics, visualizer, logger, JSON exports, and CLI make evaluation reproducible.
AUV services and jobs move through a full process-state machine instead of a single ready queue.
NEW, READY, RUNNING, BLOCKED, WAITING, SUSPENDED, FAULT, and TERMINATED transitions.
Navigation, DepthControl, HullIntegrity, BatteryMonitor, O2Scrubber, and thermal services respawn.
Sonar, hydrophone, GPS, comms, and logging can enter WAITING for device response.
Tasks can fault, retry recovery, or terminate after repeated failures.
Four policies share the same task model but optimize different OS goals.
Weighted score = criticality + priority + aging + deadline + AI boost - execution penalty.
Runs the task with the earliest deadline; priority breaks deadline ties.
Promotes critical services and sorts by effective priority plus energy tie-break.
Fairness baseline; each ready task rotates with equal opportunity.
All schedulers use effective_priority, so AI boosts and PIP boosts are visible to dispatch logic.
The model is advisory: it predicts risk and recommends priority boosts, but the kernel still decides.
Task type, base priority, criticality, remaining time, memory availability, and system state.
Offline-trained model estimates fault probability with bounded, deterministic inference cost.
Critical task, memory pressure, and deadline proximity become explainable advisory reasons.
Fault probability is converted into a graduated boost added to effective priority.

text: Faults depend on system state, so the AI learns meaningful correlations instead of random noise.
fault risk = task base risk x memory pressure x deadline urgency x criticality x depth x temperature x mission mode
LOW to CRITICAL pressure raises allocation and runtime risk.
Negative or low slack sharply increases fault likelihood.
Deep water raises hull, seal, ballast, and sensor risks.
Hot battery cells can trigger thermal cascade faults.
The OS reacts to vehicle state, not just abstract task queues.
Transient jobs are capped and cannot consume memory reserved for core services.
8-cell pack tracks SOC, voltage sag, temperature, health, ETA, and thermal throttling.
Depth, pressure, speed, heading, sonar mode, comms strength, and hull stress update every tick.
Low-power phases suspend non-critical tasks, reduce power draw, switch sonar mode, and trigger auto-surface.
The lock manager demonstrates and fixes priority inversion, a core real-time OS problem.
PIP BOOST: LOW temporarily inherits HIGH priority
LOW holds a lock needed by HIGH, while MED can keep preempting LOW.
LOW temporarily inherits HIGH priority until it releases the lock.
LockManager tracks holders, wait queues, inversion events, and inheritance boosts.
Schedulers read effective_priority, so inherited priority changes dispatch order.
The final system can be demonstrated through the SentinelOS terminal and benchmark scripts.
Shows kernel, scheduler, BMS, navigation, comms, and AI advisory initialization.
Displays power, depth, heading, memory pressure, scheduler, and mission mode.
Insert screenshot: interactive shell with `top` dashboard or `ps` process table.
Suggested commands to capture: `python -m sentinel_os -i`, `top`, `ps`, `battery`, `sched priority`, `mission survival`.
All schedulers ran on the same seeded 200-step workload with AI enabled.
Deadline miss and completion percentage chart

End evaluation should show both what works and what remains to polish.
Scheduler, PIP, metrics, and resource modules covered
5 tests still use legacy metric/BMS assumptions
Retrain or re-export ML model with current scikit-learn
No real AUV hardware or certified RTOS timing
CLI has a richer primary shell plus older demo script to clean
SentinelOS demonstrates how AI advisory can support a deterministic microkernel simulator for autonomous AUV missions.
Combines real-time scheduling, fault injection, BMS resources, environment physics, and AI risk advice.
Results compare policies using completion rate, deadline misses, faults, energy, and context switches.
The project connects scheduling theory, priority inversion, process states, and resource control to an AUV domain.
